Name: precision_interference_unification
Title: Unified Precision Allocation and Geometric Interference Cancellation for Robust Inference
Short Hypothesis: Precision allocation and geometric interference cancellation can be unified into a single training-free framework that dynamically adjusts numerical precision while simultaneously isolating task-relevant subspaces, leading to more robust and efficient inference under distribution shifts.
Related Work: Existing work includes Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) for precision allocation and Geometric Signal Separation for interference cancellation. However, these approaches are developed independently and do not exploit their complementary benefits. Our proposal integrates these ideas into a unified framework, leveraging the spectral properties of activations to simultaneously optimize precision allocation and interference cancellation.
Abstract: We propose a unified training-free framework that combines precision allocation and geometric interference cancellation to enhance the robustness and efficiency of inference under distribution shifts. Our approach leverages the spectral properties of activations to dynamically adjust numerical precision while isolating task-relevant subspaces. Specifically, we compute the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states to determine precision requirements and identify interference directions. We then project activations onto a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace to cancel geometric interference, ensuring strict computational budgets. Theoretical bounds link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise and perturbation. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, our framework achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to isolated precision allocation and interference cancellation baselines. This work provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Evaluate on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks to measure robustness to distribution shifts.
Compare throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers with isolated precision allocation (SQR) and interference cancellation (Geometric Signal Separation) baselines.
Measure subspace alignment stability and generalization stability under quantization noise and perturbation.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the contributions of precision allocation and interference cancellation.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential computational overhead from real-time spectral diagnostics.
Possible sensitivity to hyperparameters in the projection and precision-switching mechanisms.
Limited applicability to tasks with highly non-linear interference patterns.
